evidence_quotes.quote: dark patterns, misleading marketing, addictive design

paragraphs.text: To whom it may concern, The initiative taken by legislators to improve the digital environment for EU consumers as pertaining to dark patterns, misleading marketing, addictive design and unfair personalization, is commendable. It has been necessary for a long time to take action against commercial practices that are harmful for the consumer and infringe on the privacy of EU citizens. That being the case, it is necessary to establish that the measures do not serve to further infringe upon the rights of EU citizens. The practices targeted by the act are no less egregious when applied to adults than to minors. Indeed, they only serve to improve the commercial success of products and services offered within the digital realm, without improving their quality. This leads to commercial practices that degrade the user experience and extract value from the consumer without an added benefit for them. Furthermore, they allow for taking advantage of all vulnerable consumers, regardless of age. As such, I see no reason for distinction between minors and adults as concerning the above practices. Therefore, I dont consider it necessary to increase the amount of personal data being gathered online to enforce such measures. If anything the introduction of tools online that gather more data will further degrade the online experience, violate EU citizen privacy and increase the likelihood of EU citizen data being misused online. AS SUCH, I AM WHOLLY AGAINST THE DFA INTRODUCING FURTHER AGE VERIFICATION AND AGE ESTIMATION MEASURES. Privacy is a basic right within the EU and it is being steadily threatened by both private entities and governments. Further degrading this right, especially for creating a legal distinction, where no such distinction is necessary is unacceptable. Thank you very much for your time and attention.
